10	# ADR-024: Power Escalation and Review Routing Policy
11	
12	**Status:** Accepted
13	**Date:** 2026-05-06
14	**Authors:** bead `ddx-5f1eac4f`
15	
16	## Context
17	
18	DDx now has three overlapping concerns in the bead execution path:
19	
20	- retrying a bead attempt when stronger reasoning could plausibly help
21	- running an adversarial pre-close reviewer gate that is independent from the
22	  implementer
23	- enforcing cost visibility and budget stops without taking ownership of
24	  provider/model routing
25	
26	Fizeau owns harness/provider/model routing. DDx owns bead
27	state, worktree isolation, evidence, gates, review verdicts, cooldowns, and
28	the queue-drain loop. The escalation policy therefore has to be expressed in
29	terms of DDx-owned evidence and abstract power bounds, not concrete model names.
30	
31	Earlier planning used "tier" and profile language. That vocabulary is now
32	migration debt when it implies DDx choosing a provider, model, or fallback chain.
33	The stable contract is `MinPower` / `MaxPower` plus opaque passthrough
34	constraints.
35	
36	## Decision
37	
38	DDx power escalation is a DDx retry-policy decision expressed only by changing
39	the next request's `MinPower` and carrying the same request facts forward.
40	DDx only changes `MinPower` and carries request facts forward; Fizeau chooses
41	the concrete route.
42	
43	### Primary Attempt Escalation
44	
45	For a primary bead attempt, DDx may schedule a higher-power retry only when all
46	of the following are true:
47	
48	1. a valid attempt started and produced DDx-owned evidence;
49	2. the outcome is classified as plausibly capability-sensitive; and
50	3. no stop condition, hard passthrough constraint, budget cap, cooldown, or
51	   operator-action class has already taken ownership of the result.
52	
53	Eligible capability-sensitive classes include failed post-run checks,
54	implementation-quality failures, review blocks that identify fixable reasoning
55	or design gaps, and no-changes-after-attempt when the model had valid context.
56	
57	Ineligible classes include routing/passthrough conflicts, missing tools,
58	authentication or quota failures, invalid bead metadata, dirty worktree, hard
59	merge/land conflicts that need human judgment, command-not-found setup failures,
60	and any failure where retry would require DDx to inspect or mutate
61	`--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`.
62	
63	When a higher-power retry is allowed, DDx computes the next `MinPower` floor
64	from Fizeau's catalog power numbers. It may skip catalog power floors that have
65	no viable auto-routable model, but it still sends only a new `MinPower` bound
66	to Fizeau. Fizeau chooses the concrete route and reports actual model,
67	provider, and power.
68	
69	### Passthrough Stickiness
70	
71	Operator-supplied passthrough constraints are sticky across every retry.
72	
73	If the operator supplied `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`, DDx forwards
74	the same values unchanged. If those constraints make a later power request
75	unsatisfiable, DDx records a typed terminal classification such as
76	`blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or `agent_power_unsatisfied` and reports
77	operator action required. DDx must not remove pins, widen pins, substitute a
78	fallback route, call any route-selection helper to work around the conflict, or
79	loop over concrete route names.
80	
81	### Infrastructure And Rate-Limit Fallback
82	
83	Infrastructure failures are not capability failures. Provider 5xx responses,
84	network unreachability, command-not-found, authentication failures, quota
85	exhaustion, and analogous transport/setup failures do not consume escalation
86	budget. DDx records the failure and either leaves the bead immediately
87	reclaimable or applies a retry-after cooldown only when time passing could
88	plausibly fix the same attempt class.
89	
90	HTTP 429 / rate-limit handling is internal to one attempt. The claim stays held,
91	DDx honors a parseable `Retry-After` when present, otherwise uses bounded
92	exponential backoff, and emits `rate-limit-retry` evidence. When the retry
93	budget is exhausted, the attempt falls back to the ordinary `execution_failed`
94	mapping.
95	
96	### Default Adversarial Pre-Close Review Gate
97	
98	Every close-eligible implementation result passes through an adversarial
99	pre-close review gate before DDx mutates the bead to `closed`. Review is enabled
100	by default. Disabling it is an explicit operator override and must record an
101	auditable reason.
102	
103	The gate runs after the implementation attempt has produced stable evidence
104	(`base_rev`, `result_rev`, diff, verification command output, bead metadata, and
105	governing artifacts), and before the durable close mutation. The candidate
106	commit may already exist in git so the reviewer can inspect a stable
107	`result_rev`; the bead is not closed until the review gate approves that result.
108	
109	The default gate dispatches two independent reviewer invocations. Each reviewer
110	runs as a **read-only tool reviewer** in the still-live attempt worktree, before
111	land. The bounded evidence bundle (candidate diff, acceptance criteria, governing
112	artifacts) is the canonical review input; same-worktree read-only tool access
113	(file reads, searches) is supplemental and does not override the bounded bundle.
114	The reviewer cannot write files or create commits. Each reviewer must return a
115	structured verdict with per-acceptance-criterion evidence.
116	
117	**Transitional single-slot allowance.** Until the two-slot quorum reviewer
118	implementation lands, a single reviewer slot satisfies the gate. The removal
119	condition is the landing of the bead that implements two-slot quorum aggregation.
120	
121	DDx requests a reviewer that is stronger than the implementer by using the
122	implementer's actual power as evidence and setting reviewer `MinPower` to a
123	higher floor. DDx also supplies structured correlation facts:
124	
125	- `role=reviewer`
126	- `bead_id`
127	- `attempt_id`
128	- `session_id`
129	- `result_rev`
130	- `review_group_id`
131	- `reviewer_index`
132	- implementer harness/provider/model/power when known
133	
134	These fields are Day-1 observability. DDx records them so operators and metrics
135	can see whether review pairing degraded. Future agent-side routing
136	intelligence may use `Role` and correlation internally, but DDx does not
137	specify that algorithm and must not depend on it.
138	
139	Different-provider review is best effort. If the agent returns a reviewer on
140	the same provider as the implementer, DDx emits a
141	`review-pairing-degraded` event. That event affects triage bias and operator
142	visibility; it is not by itself a review failure.
143	
144	### Review Outcomes And Retry
145	
146	Reviewer output must satisfy the structured verdict contract. A valid approval
147	requires per-AC evidence; an `APPROVE` without evidence is malformed, not a
148	pass. The aggregate gate is conservative:
149	
150	- unanimous reviewer `APPROVE` with per-AC evidence permits close;
151	- any evidenced `REQUEST_CHANGES` or `BLOCK` prevents close;
152	- reviewer disagreement is preserved in the review group evidence, and the
153	  non-approve verdict wins when it cites evidence;
154	- malformed, empty, context-overflow, and transport failures are
155	  `review-error` classes scoped to the reviewed `result_rev` and reviewer.
156	
157	Non-approve reviewer findings are classified before the next action:
158	
159	- `review_fixable_gap` — implementation or test work is missing, incomplete, or
160	  erroneous. DDx schedules a new implementation cycle on the same bead when
161	  retry budgets allow. The next implementation prompt includes the review
162	  findings as required repair context. DDx may raise `MinPower` only when the
163	  finding is plausibly capability-sensitive.
164	- `review_spec_gap` / `review_missing_acceptance` — the bead or governing spec
165	  is ambiguous, unverifiable, contradictory, or missing acceptance criteria. DDx
166	  moves the bead to `status=proposed`, clears the active claim, and does not ask
167	  another implementer to guess. The operator resolves that state by retry,
168	  split, obsolete, or defer actions recorded in the bead history.
169	- `review_too_large` — the result or bead is too broad for bounded review. DDx
170	  runs the intake/decomposition path, decomposes the parent when lossless child
171	  work is possible, or moves it to `status=proposed` when operator input is
172	  required. It does not re-run the same monolithic implementation attempt.
173	- `review_unsafe_or_out_of_scope` — the implementation changed forbidden scope,
174	  removed checks, weakened behavior, or otherwise needs explicit repair. If the
175	  repair is mechanical it follows `review_fixable_gap`; otherwise it moves to
176	  `status=proposed`.
177	
178	Review errors retry the review path up to `review_max_retries_per_candidate` for
179	the same candidate ref (`result_rev`) and reviewer slot. On exhaustion, DDx
180	emits `review-manual-required`, clears the active claim, moves the bead to
181	`status=proposed`, and leaves it for operator review without closing it. This
182	terminal resolution is the same contract used by `review_spec_gap` and
183	`review_missing_acceptance`: the bead carries the human-review context and waits
184	for an operator decision rather than another automatic implementation attempt. A
185	new candidate ref (from a repair cycle or a fresh `ddx try`) resets this counter
186	independently; it is strictly per-candidate. A new implementation result starts a
187	new review-error retry scope, while
188	`review_fixable_gap` continues through the normal repair retry path counted
189	against `repair_max_cycles` instead of entering the operator lane.
190	
191	Each implementation/review cycle is append-only. Repair commits in the worktree
192	are also append-only — no `git reset`, `git commit --amend`, `git squash`, or
193	`git rebase` against the prior candidate is permitted in a repair cycle. A
194	repair attempt creates a new cycle record linked to the prior review group
195	(`repair_context_from_review_group`) and records its own `base_rev`,
196	`result_rev`, implementer run ids, verification output, reviewer run ids,
197	aggregate verdict, retry/decomposition/block decision, and cost summary. No
198	cycle overwrites prior evidence.
199	
200	### Cost Accounting
201	
202	Primary attempts and review attempts both report usage and cost into the same
203	run evidence stream. FEAT-014 defines the normalized cost signal fields.
204	FEAT-010 owns the queue-drain budget stop. Subscription-bundled and local
205	providers may be excluded from billed-cost caps by explicit cost-class metadata;
206	unknown cost class counts by default.
207	
208	Reviewer cost is not special. When a review invocation reports billable cost,
209	that cost contributes to the same drain-level cap as implementation attempts.
210	If the cap is reached, DDx stops claiming more work and records an observable
211	budget stop instead of disguising the stop as model failure.
212	
213	## Consequences
214	
215	- FEAT-006 owns request construction: `MinPower` / `MaxPower`, passthrough
216	  envelope, role/correlation metadata, and actual power recording.
217	- FEAT-010 owns intake, retry scheduling, stop-condition evaluation,
218	  no-progress handling, adversarial review aggregation, review retry scopes, and
219	  budget stops.
220	- FEAT-014 owns normalized usage, cost, cost-class, and freshness semantics.
221	- FEAT-022 and TD-033 own review evidence assembly and read-only tool reviewer mode.
222	- DDx routing lint should treat concrete route mutation in `run`, `try`, or
223	  `work` policy as a regression.
224	
225	## Non-Goals
226	
227	- Specifying Fizeau's internal routing, fallback, provider-health, or model
228	  scoring algorithm.
229	- Guaranteeing a different reviewer provider or model. DDx can request stronger
230	  review, dispatch independent reviewer slots, and record degradation, not force
231	  a route.
232	- Retrying by concrete model name or profile mutation.
233	- Adding a fourth run layer or a review-specific execution substrate.
234	
235	## References
236	
237	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md`
238	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-task-execution.md`
239	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-014-token-awareness.md`
240	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-022-prompt-evidence-assembly.md`
241	- `docs/migrations/routing-config.md`
242	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-033-multi-turn-structured-evidence-assembly.md`

9	# Technical Design: Bead State Machine and Naming-Role Decisions
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This TD is the canonical reference for what bead state means in DDx and where
14	each observed name in the codebase belongs. It exists because schema, docs,
15	code, and persisted data have drifted: the bead-record JSON Schema enumerates
16	six statuses (the bd/br canonical set), FEAT-004 still documents three, and
17	several legacy/backcompat names appear in code paths (`done`, `needs_human`,
18	`pending`, `ready`, `review`, `needs_investigation`) without a single decision
19	record explaining whether each is a status, a derived queue category, an
20	event kind, a label, or worker state.
21	
22	The five in-flight hygiene beads each introduce new state-machine vocabulary:
23	
24	- ddx-b24e9630 — `no_changes_*` outcome verification (NoChangesContract)
25	- ddx-3c154349 — auto-triage of stuck beads (TriageContract)
26	- ddx-aede917d — drain pause on quota exhaustion (QuotaPauseContract)
27	- ddx-c6e3db02 — rate-limit retry behavior (RateLimitRetryContract)
28	- ddx-da11a34a — main-git/tracker lock contention handling
29	  (LockContentionContract)
30	
31	Without one TD that nails down the categories and the transition matrix,
32	those five beads will each independently invent overlapping vocabulary.
33	
34	This TD authors the design only. The mechanical reconciliation
35	(schema-vs-FEAT-004 alignment, code rename of non-status names, CI guards,
36	hygiene-bead AC substitution, migration survey) is filed as sibling beads.
37	
38	## Critical Constraint (per ADR-004)
39	
40	The bead-record envelope must remain compatible with the bd/br interchange
41	contract. The persisted `status` enum is **fixed** at the bd/br canonical six
42	values:
43	
44	```
45	open, in_progress, closed, blocked, proposed, cancelled
46	```
47	
48	DDx-specific execution semantics live in **labels**, **events**, or the
49	preserved-extras **Extra** map — never in new statuses. Adding a new persisted
50	status requires upstream bd/br coordination plus an ADR-004 amendment. This
51	TD does not authorize any such addition.
52	
53	## Today's Observed State (verified)
54	
55	- `cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json` enumerates six values.
56	- `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-beads.md` line 65 still documents
57	  three values (`open, in_progress, closed`). FEAT-004 lags the schema.
58	- `ddx bead list --json | jq -r '.[].status' | sort -u` on real projects
59	  returns only `open`, `in_progress`, `closed` for active rows today —
60	  `blocked`, `proposed`, and `cancelled` are schema-allowed but unused in
61	  the current active queue.
62	- The legacy/backcompat names `done`, `needs_human`, `pending`, `ready`, `review`,
63	  `needs_investigation` appear in code paths but are **not** persisted bead
64	  statuses today. Each falls into one of the other categories defined below.
65	
66	## Sections
67	
68	The remainder of this TD is structured as the eleven design sections required
69	by the originating bead, followed by five per-hygiene-bead contract
70	subsections.
71	
72	---
73	
74	## 1. Category Taxonomy
75	
76	Every state-machine name observed in DDx falls into exactly one of the
77	following categories. The category determines where the name is allowed to
78	live (schema, label namespace, event kind, queue derivation, or worker
79	process state).
80	
81	| Category | Storage location | Lifecycle | Owner |
82	|---|---|---|---|
83	| Persisted bead status | `status` field on the bead record | Mutated by atomic snapshot rewrite | Bead store, locked to bd/br set |
84	| Derived queue category | Computed on read from status + deps + preserved metadata | Never persisted | Queue derivation code (`ddx bead ready/blocked/status`) |
85	| Event kind | Append-only entry in `Extra["events"][].kind` | Append-only; explains state, does not control lifecycle | Drain loop, agent service, CLI |
86	| Terminal phase | A persisted `closed` status plus a closing event/label that names *why* | Mutated once on close | Drain loop / CLI |
87	| Claim metadata | `assignee`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid` fields (preserved extras) | Set on claim, cleared on unclaim, expired by triage | Claim resolution path (TD-004) |
88	| Label | Entry in the `labels` array | Explains or filters state; never controls lifecycle | Anyone with `ddx bead update` |
89	| Extra metadata field | Arbitrary key under preserved extras | Explains or filters state; never controls lifecycle | Subsystem owning that key |
90	| Worker state | In-memory state of the drain process | Lives only for the worker's lifetime | Drain loop process |
91	
92	A name MUST NOT span categories. If a name today appears as both a label and
93	a derived queue category (e.g., `ready` is derived; `ready` MUST NOT also
94	appear as a label), the sibling reconciliation bead removes the duplicate
95	usage.
96	
97	## 2. Persisted Status Enumeration
98	
99	The persisted bead status is exactly:
100	
101	```
102	open | in_progress | closed | blocked | proposed | cancelled
103	```
104	
105	This is the bd/br-canonical set; it matches `bead-record.schema.json`. This
106	TD does **not** add, rename, or remove any persisted status.
107	
108	Plain-English semantics:
109	
110	- `open` — accepted active work. No claim is active. This includes work that is
111	  waiting on dependencies; dependency waiting is derived from the dependency DAG
112	  while the persisted status remains `open`.
113	- `in_progress` — the bead has an active claim (`assignee`, `claimed-at`,
114	  `claimed-pid` populated). The drain loop or an operator has taken
115	  ownership.
116	- `closed` — terminal satisfied work. A `closed` dependency satisfies
117	  downstream beads. The reason for closure is encoded in a closing event and/or
118	  label; the status itself does not carry the reason.
119	- `blocked` — accepted work paused by a rare external, recheckable blocker.
120	  Dependency waits are not `status=blocked`; they are derived from unsatisfied
121	  dependencies while the bead remains `open`.
122	- `proposed` — operator decision required. Proposed beads are not
123	  autonomous-work eligible until an operator accepts, rewrites, splits, waives,
124	  or cancels them.
125	- `cancelled` — terminal not-doing. Distinct from `closed`: `cancelled` does
126	  not satisfy dependents unless a later explicit dependency policy says
127	  otherwise.
128	
129	## 3. Transition Matrix
130	
131	| From → To | Allowed? | Driver | Event fired |
132	|---|---|---|---|
133	| `proposed` → `open` | yes | operator (`ddx bead update --status open`) | `triaged` |
134	| `proposed` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
135	| `open` → `proposed` | yes | readiness or operator found missing decision input | `triage-ambiguous` / `review-manual-required` |
136	| `open` → `in_progress` | yes | drain loop or operator (claim) | `claimed` |
137	| `open` → `blocked` | yes | operator or auto-triage found an external recheckable blocker | `blocked` |
138	| `open` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
139	| `in_progress` → `open` | yes | unclaim (operator or stale-claim sweep) | `unclaimed` |
140	| `in_progress` → `closed` | yes | drain loop (on merge/already-satisfied) or operator | `closed-merged` / `closed-already-satisfied` |
141	| `in_progress` → `proposed` | yes | non-automatable review/readiness finding or exhausted repair/review budget | `review-block` / `review-manual-required` |
142	| `in_progress` → `blocked` | yes | drain loop or operator found an external recheckable blocker | `blocked` |
143	| `blocked` → `open` | yes | operator (block resolved) | `unblocked` |
144	| `blocked` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
145	| `closed` → * | no | — | — (closed is terminal) |
146	| `cancelled` → * | no | — | — (cancelled is terminal) |
147	
148	Closed and cancelled are terminal. `closed` satisfies dependency edges;
149	`cancelled` does not satisfy dependency edges unless a future dependency policy
150	explicitly defines an exception. Re-opening a closed bead is not a transition;
151	it is filing a follow-up bead with `replaces` set.
152	
153	### 3.1 Derived Queue Buckets
154	
155	Persisted status is the sole DDx-owned lifecycle field. Queue buckets are
156	computed read models over status, dependency edges, claim metadata, and
157	preserved Extra fields:
158	
159	- `execution-ready` — `status=open`, no active claim, every dependency is
160	  `closed`, and no execution-suppressing metadata is present.
161	- `dependency-waiting` — `status=open` with at least one dependency that is not
162	  `closed`; this is derived waiting, not `status=blocked`.
163	- `operator-review` — `status=proposed`; these beads require an operator
164	  decision before autonomous execution.
165	- `externally-blocked` — `status=blocked`; the blocker must be external and
166	  recheckable.
167	- `active` — `status=in_progress`; claim metadata names the current owner.
168	- `terminal-satisfied` — `status=closed`; this satisfies dependency edges.
169	- `terminal-not-doing` — `status=cancelled`; this does not satisfy dependency
170	  edges unless a future dependency policy explicitly says otherwise.
171	
172	## 4. Event Vocabulary (unified)
173	
174	Events are append-only entries on `Extra["events"]` (see TD-004 evidence
175	model). The kinds below form the closed vocabulary used across the five
176	hygiene beads. New kinds require updating this section.
177	
178	Lifecycle events:
179	
180	- `triaged` — `proposed → open`
181	- `claimed` — claim acquired
182	- `unclaimed` — claim released
183	- `blocked` — moved to `blocked` (carries a reason in `body`)
184	- `unblocked` — moved off `blocked`
185	- `closed-merged` — drain loop closed after a merge
186	- `closed-already-satisfied` — drain loop detected the work was already done
187	- `closed` — operator-driven close (catch-all)
188	- `cancelled` — moved to `cancelled`
189	
190	Drain-outcome events (no_changes family — NoChangesContract):
191	
192	- `no_changes_verified` — the attempt produced no commit, but supplied a
193	  `verification_command` that passed; the bead is already satisfied.
194	- `no_changes_unverified` — the attempt produced no commit and supplied a
195	  `verification_command`, but the command failed or could not run.
196	- `no_changes_unjustified` — the attempt produced no commit without enough
197	  structured rationale to prove either satisfaction or a durable blocker.
198	- `no_changes_needs_investigation` — legacy/backcompat event name for an
199	  attempt that explicitly asked for operator triage; maps to `status=proposed`
200	  when operator input is required.
201	- `no_changes_decomposed` — agent decomposed the bead instead of changing
202	  files.
203	- `no_changes_blocked` — agent declared no_changes with a justified external
204	  blocker.
205	- `no_changes_recoverable` — transient cause; retrying the same bead after
206	  time passes can plausibly succeed.
207	
208	Drain-control events:
209	
210	- `drain-paused-quota` — QuotaPauseContract: drain paused because the active
211	  harness reported quota exhaustion
212	- `drain-resumed-quota`
213	- `rate-limit-retry` — RateLimitRetryContract: a single attempt was retried
214	  after a rate-limit response
215	- `lock-contention` — LockContentionContract: store-lock contention was
216	  observed and handled (retry or backoff)
217	
218	Review and triage events:
219	
220	- `review-block` — reviewer raised a BLOCKING finding
221	- `review-pass` — reviewer cleared the change
222	- `review-request-changes` — one or more reviewer slots returned a structured
223	  `REQUEST_CHANGES` verdict; body carries the review group id and per-AC findings
224	- `review-fixable-gap` — reviewer found an implementation/test gap that can be
225	  repaired by another automated cycle
226	- `review-too-large` — reviewer or readiness found the bead/result too broad for a
227	  bounded review cycle
228	- `review-error` — reviewer invocation failed, was malformed, overflowed, or
229	  returned no parseable verdict
230	- `review-manual-required` — reviewer failures or non-automatable findings
231	  exhausted automatic recovery and require operator action
232	- `triage-decomposed` — readiness or review decomposed a parent into child beads
233	- `triage-overflow` — decomposition reached the depth cap
234	- `triage-rewritten` / `intake-rewritten` — readiness applied a validated
235	  replacement rewrite or metadata update before claim; body carries changed
236	  fields, rationale, before/after hashes or attachment pointers, and preservation
237	  evidence
238	- `triage-ambiguous` — readiness could not safely clarify the bead
239	- `auto-triage` — TriageContract: triage path mutated labels/status
240	
241	Candidate-cycle events (FEAT-010 candidate-cycle pipeline):
242	
243	- `candidate-pinned` — layer 2 pinned a candidate ref for the current cycle's
244	  `result_rev` before dispatching reviewers; body carries `candidate_ref`,
245	  `cycle_index`, and `attempt_id`
246	- `candidate-checks-failed` — post-run verification (tests, lint, gates) failed
247	  against the candidate; body carries check command and exit status
248	- `repair-cycle-started` — a repair cycle has been initiated in the still-live
249	  worktree; body carries `cycle_index`, `repair_context_from_review_group`, and
250	  `attempt_id`
251	- `repair-cycle-exhausted` — `repair_max_cycles` was reached for this attempt;
252	  bead moves to `status=proposed`
253	- `approved-land-conflict` — the candidate received unanimous `APPROVE` but merge
254	  to the base branch conflicted; bead is re-queued at its base revision without
255	  blame on the implementer or reviewer
256	- `final-result-landed` — the approved candidate has been merged to the base
257	  branch; immediately precedes the `closed-merged` lifecycle event
258	
259	Push-outcome events:
260	
261	- `push-failed` — the commit was created but the push to the remote was rejected
262	  (branch protection, auth-token expiry, pre-push test failure, or similar);
263	  body carries stderr detail and base-rev. This outcome MUST NOT trigger
264	  `execute-loop-retry-after` — the cause requires operator action or a code fix,
265	  not time elapsed (see section 5 invariant).
266	- `push-conflict` — the push was rejected because the remote advanced since the
267	  claim was acquired (non-fast-forward); body carries the current remote head.
268	  A brief recheckable cooldown is appropriate (see section 5).
269	
270	Each event SHOULD include `kind`, `actor`, `created_at`, and a free-form
271	`body` (TD-004 schema). Drain events SHOULD additionally include the
272	`run-id` of the execution attempt in `body` or in a structured `extra`
273	field.
274	
275	## 5. Outcome → Label / Event / Extra Mapping
276	
277	When a drain attempt finishes, `execute-bead` returns one of a fixed set of
278	outcomes plus optional no_changes lifecycle evidence. The mapping below is the
279	canonical queue-management contract.
280	
281	Lifecycle reliability invariants:
282	
283	- Raw `no_changes` is attempt evidence, not a durable bead queue state. The
284	  drain loop MUST translate it into one of the rows below before mutating the
285	  bead.
286	- `execute-loop-retry-after` may be set only when retrying the same bead after
287	  time passes could plausibly succeed without human/spec/dependency changes.
288	  Outcomes that MUST NOT set `execute-loop-retry-after` (cause is not
289	  time-resolvable; requires operator action or a code fix):
290	  `push_failed`, `declined_needs_decomposition`, `review_spec_gap`,
291	  `review_missing_acceptance`, and `review_too_large` at the decomposition depth
292	  cap.
293	  Outcomes that MAY set `execute-loop-retry-after` (recheckable by waiting):
294	  `push_conflict` (15 min — remote advanced; re-fetch and re-attempt resolves
295	  naturally), `no_viable_provider` (15 min — provider may become available),
296	  `land_conflict` (15 min — base branch may advance to a clean merge point), and
297	  transient infra/quota/transport (15 min — transient condition may clear).
298	  Cooldown lifetime SHOULD match the recheckable window; 15 min is the ceiling
299	  for every current recheckable outcome. A 24 h cooldown is never appropriate
300	  for any outcome in this table.
301	- Every bead excluded from ordinary `ddx work` execution MUST have an
302	  explainable durable reason using existing mechanisms: dependency edge,
303	  `proposed` status, external `blocked` status, `execution-eligible=false`,
304	  `superseded-by`, epic/parent queue mode, or an active retry cooldown. Labels,
305	  events, and Extra fields may explain that reason but do not control
306	  lifecycle.
307	- `closed` means implementation, verification, and the default adversarial
308	  pre-close review gate have all passed, or the work was verified as already
309	  satisfied. Review failure never reopens a closed bead; it prevents close.
310	- Automatic implementation retry is bounded and applies only to classifications
311	  where the implementer had valid task context and further automated work can
312	  plausibly resolve the finding. Spec gaps, missing acceptance criteria,
313	  decomposition overflow, and exhausted reviewer failures require operator
314	  resolution.
315	- Latest terminal events and close evidence beat stale `execute-loop-*` Extra
316	  metadata. Reconciliation may clear stale management fields, but MUST preserve
317	  append-only events and evidence.
318	- `ClosureGate` (store.go:1245, inside `closeWithEvidence`) applies exclusively
319	  to evidence-bearing execute-bead closes via `CloseWithEvidence`. The
320	  dependency-satisfied reconcile-close path (`applyReconcilePlan` at
321	  reconcile.go:208-254, `CloseSatisfied=true`) is a distinct meta-close that
322	  intentionally bypasses `ClosureGate`: the bead has no execution session and no
323	  `closing_commit_sha` of its own. The bypass is safe because every transitive
324	  dependency is `closed` — each having individually passed `ClosureGate` or been
325	  a prior meta-close — so the parent's closure inherits its evidence by reference
326	  through the dependency edges.
327	
328	| Outcome or lifecycle action | Status transition | Label changes | Events appended | Extra updates |
329	|---|---|---|---|---|
330	| `review_pass` after merged candidate | `in_progress → closed` | remove `claimed`, add `last-merged-rev:<sha>` (optional) | `review-pass` + `closed-merged` | `Extra["last-run"]`, `Extra["last-review"]`, and `Extra["closing_commit_sha"]` updated; clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields |
331	| `already_satisfied` | `in_progress → closed` | (none) | `closed-already-satisfied` | `Extra["last-run"]`; clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields |
332	| `review_fixable_gap` with retry budget remaining | no durable status change if continuing immediately; otherwise `in_progress → open` (claim released) | no lifecycle label required | `review-fixable-gap` + retry decision | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref; next cycle records `repair_context_from_review_group` |
333	| `review_fixable_gap` with retry budget exhausted | `in_progress → proposed` | optional explanatory review label only | `review-block` | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref and exhausted budget |
334	| `review_spec_gap` / `review_missing_acceptance` | `in_progress → proposed` | add `triage:spec-gap` or `triage:missing-acceptance` as explanatory labels | `review-block` | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref |
335	| `review_too_large` | `in_progress → open` after children and dependency edges are filed, or `in_progress → proposed` at depth cap/lossy split | add `decomposed` when children exist | `review-too-large` and optionally `triage-decomposed` / `triage-overflow` | `Extra["children"]` and AC mapping when decomposed |
336	| `review_error` below retry cap | no durable status change; retry reviewer for same `result_rev` | (none) | `review-error` | `Extra["last-review-error"]` carries class, reviewer slot, and attempt count |
337	| `review_error` exhausted | `in_progress → proposed` | optional explanatory review label only | `review-manual-required` | `Extra["last-review-error"]` carries class, reviewer slot, and attempt count |
338	| `execution_failed` | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `unclaimed` + a structured failure event | `Extra["last-run"]` |
339	| verified no_changes already satisfied | `in_progress → closed` | remove no_changes triage labels if present | `no_changes_verified` + `closed-already-satisfied` | `Extra["last-run"]`; clear `execute-loop-retry-after`, `execute-loop-last-status`, and `execute-loop-last-detail` |
340	| unverified no_changes | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:no-changes-unverified` | `no_changes_unverified` | record verification command/result; do not set retry cooldown by default |
341	| unjustified no_changes / no rationale | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:no-changes-unjustified` | `no_changes_unjustified` | record rationale absence/detail; do not set retry cooldown by default |
342	| legacy no_changes investigation because work is too large | `in_progress → open` after orchestrator child beads and dependency edges are filed, or `in_progress → proposed` at queue-level depth cap/lossy split | add `decomposed` when children exist | legacy/backcompat `no_changes_needs_investigation` + `triage-decomposed` or `triage-overflow` | `Extra["last-rationale"]`, `Extra["children"]`, and AC mapping; do not set retry cooldown |
343	| legacy no_changes investigation for non-decomposition reason | `in_progress → proposed` when operator action is required before retry; otherwise `in_progress → open` (claim released) | optional explanatory triage labels only | legacy/backcompat `no_changes_needs_investigation` | `Extra["last-rationale"]`; do not set retry cooldown by default |
344	| parent/epic/decomposed container | `in_progress → open` with dependency edges to children when children were created; otherwise `in_progress → open` with `execution-eligible=false` | add `decomposed` when children exist | `no_changes_decomposed` or `triage-decomposed` | `Extra["children"]` lists child IDs plus AC mapping, or `Extra["execution-eligible"]=false` explains container-only work |
345	| external blocker | `in_progress → blocked` for hard external recheckable blockers, or `in_progress → open` for visible soft blockers | add `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` as explanatory label when useful | `no_changes_blocked` | `Extra["last-rationale"]` names the external blocker |
346	| superseded work | no terminal success; leave open only if visible history is needed | add no triage labels | structured superseded event if appended by caller | `Extra["superseded-by"]` names the replacement and makes ordinary execution ineligible |
347	| transient infra/quota/transport | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `no_changes_recoverable`, `drain-paused-quota`, `rate-limit-retry`, or structured transport event | may set `execute-loop-retry-after` only for the retryable time-based condition |
348	| `push_failed` (branch protection, auth-token expiry, pre-push test failure, or executor data race) | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | optional explanatory label (e.g., `blocked-on-branch-protection`) | `push-failed` with stderr detail and base-rev | `Extra["last-run"]` only; DO NOT set `execute-loop-retry-after` — the cause requires operator action or a code fix, not time elapsed |
349	| `push_conflict` (remote advanced; fast-forward rejected) | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `push-conflict` with current remote head | may set `execute-loop-retry-after` at 15 min (recheckable — remote advanced; re-fetch and re-attempt resolves naturally) |
350	| stale no_changes tracker metadata | no status change unless latest terminal evidence closes the bead | remove stale no_changes triage labels only when contradicted by terminal evidence | preserve historical events; append reconciliation event if performed | clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields when latest terminal event or close evidence proves they are obsolete |
351	| dependency-satisfied reconcile close (`ddx bead reconcile`, `CloseSatisfied=true`; see reconcile.go:208-254) | `open → closed` via `UpdateWithLifecycleStatus`, `ManualClose=true`; `ClosureGate` is **not** invoked on this path (see store.go:1245 and the invariant above) | remove stale `execute-loop-*` labels if present; add `reconciled-nochanges-state` label | `lifecycle_reconciled` | `Extra["execute-loop-*"]` management fields cleared per `ReconcilePlan.ClearFields`; `externalizeEvents` called at reconcile.go:252 after close |
352	
353	Legacy/backcompat `needs_human` and `triage:needs-investigation` labels are not
354	lifecycle controls. New routing uses `status=proposed` for operator decisions;
355	those labels may remain only as migration metadata until cleanup removes them.
356	
357	## 6. Claim Semantics
358	
359	Claim is metadata, not status. The persisted-status convention is that
360	`in_progress` implies a claim is held; `open` implies no claim. The actual
361	claim fields are:
362	
363	- `Extra["assignee"]` (string) — the entity holding the claim.
364	- `Extra["claimed-at"]` (RFC3339 timestamp) — when the claim was acquired.
365	- `Extra["claimed-pid"]` (int) — process id of the claim holder, advisory.
366	
367	Acquired:
368	
369	- During `open → in_progress` transition under the store lock.
370	- The drain loop and operator CLI (`ddx bead update --claim`) are the two
371	  legitimate drivers.
372	
373	Released:
374	
375	- Explicit `--unclaim` (or implicit unclaim on `in_progress → open`).
376	- On terminal close (`closed` / `cancelled`) the claim metadata is left in
377	  place as historical record but is no longer authoritative.
378	
379	Worker shutdown and interruption:
380	
381	- A worker that receives graceful shutdown while no bead attempt has reached a
382	  terminal mutation MUST release any active claim it owns before exiting. The
383	  bead returns `in_progress → open`, claim metadata is cleared, and an
384	  `unclaimed` event records `reason=worker_shutdown`.
385	- If the worker has already applied a terminal mutation (`closed`,
386	  `cancelled`, or `blocked`) for the current attempt, shutdown MUST NOT undo
387	  that terminal state. The worker may emit best-effort worker-disconnect
388	  telemetry, but bead state is already authoritative.
389	- If the child agent process or attempt worktree is interrupted by context
390	  cancellation, SIGTERM, SIGINT, or a server/operator cancel before a terminal
391	  mutation, the attempt is classified as mechanically disrupted. The worker
392	  preserves any available evidence, appends a structured attempt/interruption
393	  event, releases the claim, and leaves the bead re-claimable unless a separate
394	  explicit blocker or retryable cooldown was recorded.
395	- An ungraceful worker death may strand an `in_progress` bead temporarily. The
396	  stale-claim sweep is the recovery path: after the configured stale threshold,
397	  it releases the claim, appends the recovery event, and makes the bead
398	  queue-eligible again. This recovery MUST NOT delete the original attempt
399	  events or evidence bundle.
400	- Cooldown is not a shutdown-cleanup mechanism. A stopped or interrupted worker
401	  may set `execute-loop-retry-after` only when the recorded outcome is a
402	  retryable time-based condition; ordinary shutdown, SIGTERM/SIGINT, or
403	  operator cancel releases the claim without parking the bead behind time.
404	
405	Stale claim handling (TriageContract owns the policy):
406	
407	- A claim is *stale* if `claimed-at` is older than the configured stale
408	  threshold AND the bead is still `in_progress`.
409	- Stale claims are released by the auto-triage path (`auto-triage` event),
410	  status moves `in_progress → open`, and the bead becomes queue-eligible
411	  again.
412	- Auto-triage MUST NOT delete prior claim metadata; it appends an event
413	  recording the release.
414	
415	## 7. Naming-Role Decision Matrix
416	
417	Every name observed in code, schema, docs, or persisted data is assigned
418	a single category here. Names not in the persisted-status set MUST NOT
419	appear as `status` values.
420	
421	| Name | Category | Rationale |
422	|---|---|---|
423	| `open` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; queue-eligible default. |
424	| `in_progress` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; implies an active claim. |
425	| `closed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal-success path. |
426	| `blocked` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; accepted work paused by an external recheckable blocker. |
427	| `proposed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; operator decision required before autonomous work. |
428	| `cancelled` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal not-doing path that does not satisfy dependents. |
429	| `done` | Removed alias | Historical alias of `closed` in code paths. Not a persisted status. The reconciliation sibling bead replaces remaining code references with `closed`. |
430	| `pending` / `waiting` | Derived queue category | Used by queue derivation to mean "open AND has unmet deps"; this is derived dependency waiting, not `status=blocked`. Never persisted. |
431	| `ready` | Derived queue category | "open AND no unmet deps AND not claimed". Computed from status+deps+claim; never persisted. |
432	| `review` | Terminal phase / event | A *phase of work*, not a status. Implemented as the `review-block` / `review-pass` event pair plus review evidence. Never persisted as a status. |
433	| `needs_human` | Legacy/backcompat label | Migration-only signal formerly used for operator intervention. New lifecycle routing uses `status=proposed`; this label never controls lifecycle. |
434	| `needs_investigation` | Legacy/backcompat label | Migration-only signal formerly used for unclear cause. New lifecycle routing uses `status=proposed` when operator action is required. |
435	| `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` | Label | Parameterized label naming an external upstream blocker. Distinct from derived dependency waiting and from the `blocked` status; it explains state but does not control lifecycle. |
436	| `decomposed` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_decomposed`; pairs with `Extra["children"]`. |
437	| `triage` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_no_evidence` or by auto-triage. |
438	| `idle` / `draining` / `paused-quota` / `paused-rate-limit` / `exiting` | Worker state | Lives in the drain loop process, not on the bead. See section 10. |
439	
440	## 8. Per-Hygiene-Bead Contracts
441	
442	Each hygiene bead's AC must cite this section. Anything not in the
443	contract here is out of scope for that bead.
444	
445	### 8.1 NoChangesContract (ddx-b24e9630)
446	
447	NoChangesContract outcomes use the canonical mapping in section 5. This section
448	exists only to bind the hygiene bead to that mapping; it does not define a
449	second disposition table.
450	
451	Claim behavior: the claim is released for every no_changes action that leaves
452	the bead `open`. It is not released separately when the same mutation moves the
453	bead directly to terminal `closed` or hard `blocked`.
454	
455	Loop interaction: the try package parses and verifies no_changes rationale,
456	then returns the lifecycle action. The drain loop applies section 5 exactly and
457	appends one of the no_changes event kinds from section 4; it does not invent
458	new event kinds or use cooldown as a generic parking lot.
459	
460	### 8.2 Bead Readiness Assessment And Triage Contract (ddx-3c154349)
461	
462	Bead readiness assessment is the canonical pre-claim decision for
463	actionability and scope. It owns the readiness queue decision and runs before a
464	worker owns the bead, so most readiness actions start from `open`.
465	Lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
466	triage is a separate after-evidence queue action. The implementation entrypoint
467	may still be named `MODE: intake` for compatibility, but that is legacy
468	wording only; the product concept is bead readiness assessment.
469	
470	Status transitions used:
471	
472	- `in_progress → open` when releasing stale claims.
473	- `open → open` when readiness applies a validated replacement rewrite or
474	  metadata-only safe improvement before implementation. The bead remains
475	  execution-ready unless a later readiness decision parks it.
476	- `open → blocked` when triage decides a bead has an external recheckable
477	  blocker.
478	- `open → open` when readiness decomposes a parent and adds dependency edges to
479	  children; the parent is dependency-waiting but remains `open`.
480	- `open → proposed` when readiness reaches decomposition depth overflow or finds
481	  ambiguity that cannot be safely rewritten.
482	
483	For successful replacement rewrites, the bead body may be materially shorter or
484	longer than the original when prompt fitness requires it. Preservation is proven
485	by the `triage-rewritten` / `intake-rewritten` evidence record and durable
486	anchors, not by keeping old text inside the prompt body. For rejected rewrites,
487	readiness moves the bead to `status=proposed` instead of releasing it back into
488	the execution-ready lane.
489	
490	Labels added: `triage`, `blocked-on-upstream:<id>`, `decomposed`,
491	`triage:spec-gap`, `triage:missing-acceptance` (as applicable).
492	Legacy/backcompat labels such as `needs_human` and `needs-human-decomposition`
493	may be read during migration but must not be added as lifecycle controls.
494	
495	Labels removed: `triage` after a triaged bead is reclaimed cleanly (the
496	reverse mutation of `triage` is "next successful claim removes the
497	label").
498	
499	Events fired: `auto-triage`, `triage-ambiguous`, `triage-decomposed`,
500	`triage-overflow`, `unclaimed`, optionally `blocked`.
501	
502	Claim behavior: the auto-triage sweep is the only path that releases a
503	claim it does not own. It MUST log the prior `assignee` and `claimed-at`
504	in the appended event body.
505	
506	Loop interaction: readiness and triage run out-of-band of execute-bead. They
507	MUST NOT race with an active drain attempt holding the store lock — they
508	acquire the same store lock and releases-then-reclaims is not their job.
509	
510	### 8.3 QuotaPauseContract (ddx-aede917d)
511	
512	Status transitions used: none. Quota is a worker-state concern, not a
513	bead-state concern.
514	
515	Labels: none added on the affected bead. The current attempt is treated
516	as `execution_failed` (claim released, status returns to `open`).
517	
518	Events fired: `drain-paused-quota` is appended to a worker-scoped event
519	log (drain-process record), not to the bead's event stream. A
520	`unclaimed` event is appended to the bead.
521	
522	Claim behavior: the claim on the in-flight bead is released cleanly so
523	another worker (or a later resumed worker) can pick it up.
524	
525	Loop interaction: the worker transitions to worker-state
526	`paused-quota` (section 10). It stops claiming new beads until the
527	configured backoff elapses or an explicit resume signal arrives.
528	
529	### 8.4 RateLimitRetryContract (ddx-c6e3db02)
530	
531	Status transitions used: none. Rate-limit retry is internal to a single
532	attempt; the bead remains `in_progress` throughout.
533	
534	Labels: none.
535	
536	Events fired: `rate-limit-retry` per retry, on the bead's event stream.
537	Each event carries the retry count and the wait duration in the body.
538	
539	Claim behavior: claim is held continuously across retries.
540	
541	Loop interaction: rate-limit handling is bounded by the retry budget; on
542	budget exhaustion the outcome becomes `execution_failed` and the
543	standard mapping in section 5 applies. The worker briefly enters
544	`paused-rate-limit` for the wait window, then returns to `draining`.
545	
546	### 8.5 LockContentionContract (ddx-da11a34a)
547	
548	Status transitions used: none. Lock contention is a main-git/tracker
549	coordination concern, not a bead-state concern.
550	
551	Labels: none.
552	
553	Events fired: `lock-contention` (append-only, on the affected bead, only
554	after the contention has been resolved and the mutation succeeded — so a
555	future audit can see that contention was observed). Pure read-side
556	contention does not emit an event.
557	
558	Claim behavior: unaffected. The claim is acquired or not; partial states
559	are not persisted.
560	
561	Loop interaction: the worker retries the locked operation with
562	exponential backoff up to a bounded budget. On budget exhaustion the
563	calling outcome maps to `execution_failed` (section 5). The worker does
564	not enter a dedicated worker-state for lock contention; it remains
565	`draining` and treats the failure as ordinary.
566	
567	Filesystem-shape contract: the main-git/tracker lock path
568	`.ddx/.git-tracker.lock` is a process-shared lock **directory**, not a
569	regular lockfile. Lock acquisition MUST classify the existing path
570	immediately after `mkdir` reports that it already exists and MUST NOT
571	sleep/back off until the path is confirmed to be a real lock directory.
572	
573	- Missing after race: retry acquisition immediately.
574	- Directory: apply the existing PID/`acquired_at` stale-lock policy. A
575	  directory owned by a live process remains ordinary lock contention.
576	- Stale regular file: if and only if `lstat` reports an exact regular
577	  file and its mtime is older than the stale-lock threshold, remove it
578	  with single-path removal and retry acquisition immediately.
579	- Fresh regular file: fail fast with a malformed-lock diagnostic. Do not
580	  wait for the lock-contention retry budget and do not report
581	  `owner pid: unknown`.
582	- Symlink, socket, device, or other special file: fail fast with a
583	  malformed-lock diagnostic and do not remove it.
584	
585	Malformed lock paths are operator/remediation diagnostics, not lock
586	contention. They do not emit `lock-contention`, do not introduce a new
587	status or label, and do not change claim semantics. If a malformed path
588	is surfaced through `ddx work` pre-claim guarding, the operator-facing
589	message MUST name the malformed path and expected directory shape rather
590	than repeatedly skipping every candidate behind a retry timeout.
591	
592	## 9. Future-Change Process
593	
594	This is the rule of record for any subsequent change to bead state.
595	
596	> Any bead that introduces or changes a label, an event kind, an outcome →
597	> state mapping, claim handling, or worker-state semantics MUST cite the
598	> TD-031 section that authorizes the change. If no section authorizes it,
599	> TD-031 is amended in the same PR (or a parent bead) before the dependent
600	> work lands.
601	>
602	> Adding a new persisted bead status is **not** authorized by TD-031.
603	> It requires upstream bd/br coordination AND an amendment to ADR-004.
604	
605	In practice:
606	
607	- A new event kind: amend section 4 in the same PR.
608	- A new outcome → state mapping: amend section 5.
609	- A new label: amend section 7.
610	- A new worker state: amend section 10.
611	- A new persisted status: do not start the work; file an ADR-004
612	  amendment first.
613	
614	CI guard: a sibling bead adds a check that any change to
615	`bead-record.schema.json` or to the persisted-status enum touches
616	ADR-004 and TD-031 in the same commit. That guard is out of scope for
617	this bead.
618	
619	## 10. Worker-State Enumeration
620	
621	Worker state is the in-process state of the drain loop. It is **distinct
622	from bead state** and is not persisted in the bead store. It exists only
623	for the worker's lifetime.
624	
625	| Worker state | Meaning | Entry | Exit |
626	|---|---|---|---|
627	| `idle` | Worker is up but not actively claiming. | Worker startup; transient between attempts. | Operator or scheduler moves it to `draining`. |
628	| `draining` | Worker is actively claiming and executing beads. | Default operating state. | Quota or rate-limit pause; explicit stop. |
629	| `paused-quota` | Worker has observed quota exhaustion on its harness. | `drain-paused-quota` event. | `drain-resumed-quota` event after backoff or operator resume. |
630	| `paused-rate-limit` | Worker is sleeping out a rate-limit retry window inside an attempt. | Rate-limit response from harness. | Wait window elapses; worker resumes the same attempt. |
631	| `exiting` | Worker is shutting down cleanly; will not claim more beads. | Operator stop or terminal failure. | Process exit. |
632	
633	Worker state is observable via the worker's stdout/log; it is not stored
634	on any bead. Hygiene beads that affect worker state (QuotaPauseContract,
635	RateLimitRetryContract) MUST NOT add new fields to the bead schema to
636	represent these transient states.
637	
638	## 11. One-Way Lifecycle Migration And Startup Gate
639	
640	The transition from label-owned lifecycle lanes to the status-owned lifecycle
641	is one-way. Legacy/backcompat labels and pseudo-statuses such as
642	`needs_human`, `triage:needs-investigation`, and `needs_investigation` are
643	migration input only. Normal runtime MUST NOT maintain compatibility lanes,
644	runtime aliases, or fallback queue behavior for those names after the lifecycle
645	gate is enabled.
646	
647	DDx startup MUST refuse normal operation when the active project bead queue
648	contains unmigrated lifecycle state. The preflight scans the active queue before
649	ordinary commands load queue views or mutate beads. It fails closed when it
650	finds any of:
651	
652	- open or in-progress beads carrying legacy lifecycle labels
653	  (`needs_human`, `triage:needs-investigation`, or equivalent old operator-lane
654	  labels);
655	- non-canonical pseudo-status values outside the six persisted statuses;
656	- legacy Extra fields that still control routing instead of merely preserving
657	  historical evidence.
658	
659	Allowed bypass surfaces are intentionally narrow:
660	
661	- `ddx help`, `ddx --help`, `ddx version`, and equivalent metadata-only help
662	  commands;
663	- `ddx doctor` and other read-only diagnostics that do not derive worker
664	  eligibility or mutate lifecycle;
665	- `ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run`;
666	- `ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply`.
667	
668	All other bead and worker surfaces MUST refuse to continue until migration is
669	complete, including `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`, `ddx bead status`,
670	`ddx work`, server-managed workers, GraphQL worker starts, REST/MCP worker
671	starts, and queue-readiness APIs. The refusal is a startup/configuration error,
672	not a retryable worker outcome and not an agent routing failure.
673	
674	The startup error output MUST include:
675	
676	1. counts of legacy lifecycle labels by name;
677	2. counts of non-canonical pseudo-status values by name;
678	3. the first few affected bead IDs for operator orientation;
679	4. the exact commands:
680	
681	   ```bash
682	   ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run
683	   ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply
684	   ```
685	
686	5. the rollback instruction: use git rollback (for example, restore the prior
687	   `.ddx/beads.jsonl` commit) if the one-way migration result is wrong.
688	
689	Because the bead store is git-tracked, rollback is git rollback, not dual
690	semantics. The migrator owns translating old labels and pseudo-statuses into
691	`status=proposed`, `status=open`, `status=blocked`, `status=closed`, or
692	`status=cancelled` according to this TD. Runtime code consumes only the
693	post-migration state-owned contract.

8	---
9	# Solution Design: Task Execution Lifecycle
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This design turns FEAT-010's task execution lifecycle into an implementation
14	shape. It is intentionally below the feature spec and above code-level storage
15	types: enough detail to keep the `run`, `try`, and `work` layers aligned
16	without re-opening the routing boundary in FEAT-006.
17	
18	## Layer Model
19	
20	Task execution is composed from named layers:
21	
22	| Layer | Public verb | Meaning |
23	| --- | --- | --- |
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | one upstream agent invocation |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | one bead attempt in an isolated worktree |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | one mechanical queue-drain loop |
27	
28	Each higher layer references child records from the layer beneath it. Comparison,
29	replay, benchmark, review, and artifact-regeneration workflows are compositions
30	over the task execution lifecycle, not new run kinds.
31	
32	## Record Shape
33	
34	Every record lives under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/record.json`. Large bodies are
35	attachments in the same directory.
36	
37	```json
38	{
39	  "run_id": "run_20260430T120102Z_01HV...",
40	  "layer": 2,
41	  "parent_run_id": "run_20260430T115900Z_01HV...",
42	  "started_at": "2026-04-30T12:01:02Z",
43	  "finished_at": "2026-04-30T12:04:12Z",
44	  "terminal_status": "success",
45	  "actor": "erik",
46	  "host": "sindri",
47	  "git_revision": "abc123",
48	  "ddx_version": "0.0.0-dev",
49	  "produces_artifact": null,
50	  "attachments": [
51	    {"name": "prompt", "path": "prompt.md", "media_type": "text/markdown"},
52	    {"name": "result", "path": "result.json", "media_type": "application/json"}
53	  ],
54	  "layer1": null,
55	  "layer2": {
56	    "bead_id": "ddx-12345678",
57	    "base_revision": "abc123",
58	    "result_revision": "def456",
59	    "worktree_path": ".ddx/worktrees/ddx-12345678-run_...",
60	    "finalization": "merge",
61	    "child_run_ids": ["run_20260430T120104Z_01HV..."],
62	    "gate_summary": "go test ./internal/agent"
63	  },
64	  "layer3": null
65	}
66	```
67	
68	Rules:
69	
70	- `layer` is the discriminator; exactly one of `layer1`, `layer2`, or `layer3`
71	  is populated.
72	- `parent_run_id` points upward when a run is nested; child ids live in the
73	  layer extension for efficient drill-down.
74	- `terminal_status` is the substrate outcome. Domain-specific detail remains in
75	  the layer extension and attachments.
76	- `produces_artifact` is set when a run regenerates an artifact; FEAT-007 reads
77	  it as provenance for `generated_by`.
78	- Records are append-only. Correction means writing a new record or a separate
79	  review/annotation artifact, not editing a historical record.
80	
81	## Layer Extensions
82	
83	### Layer 1: invocation atom
84	
85	Layer 1 stores the DDx request envelope and the agent's typed response fields:
86	
87	- prompt attachment or prompt reference
88	- requested `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`
89	- opaque passthrough constraints (`harness`, `provider`, `model`) when supplied
90	- non-routing work facts such as permissions, effort, timeout, and artifact id
91	- actual model and actual power returned by the agent
92	- token/cost/duration metadata
93	- upstream session id and session-log attachment pointer
94	- structured result attachment
95	
96	DDx does not persist route decisions as DDx-owned policy. Concrete
97	harness/provider/model values are recorded only as requested passthrough or
98	agent-reported facts.
99	
100	### Layer 2: bead attempt
101	
102	Layer 2 stores DDx-owned attempt orchestration:
103	
104	- bead id and governing artifact ids
105	- base revision and worktree path
106	- child layer-1 run ids
107	- commit/result revision, if produced
108	- finalization mode: `merge`, `preserve`, `no_changes`, or `aborted`
109	- preserve ref, if any
110	- gate results and review verdicts
111	- evidence-bundle attachment refs
112	- cleanup summary: removed worktree path, preserve reason, partial setup
113	  cleanup, and resource-preflight result
114	
115	Layer 2 owns success classification for a bead attempt because DDx owns commits,
116	merge/preserve state, no-change rationale, post-run gates, and review evidence.
117	The agent's exit status and actual power are inputs, not the full decision.
118	Per FEAT-010's network-free drain boundary, this layer performs no network I/O;
119	origin-sync and push are out-of-band per FEAT-023.
120	
121	### Layer 3: queue drain
122	
123	Layer 3 stores the mechanical queue loop:
124	
125	- queue snapshot at start
126	- child layer-2 attempt ids in execution order
127	- retry/escalation decisions with requested `MinPower` changes
128	- no-progress counter state
129	- stop-condition evaluation log
130	- cleanup pass summaries and resource stop details
131	- terminal disposition: `drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `no_progress`,
132	  `signal`, or `resource_exhausted`
133	
134	Layer 3 does not file new content-aware work or decide workflow policy.
135	Supervisory behavior belongs to skills or plugins layered on top.
136	Per FEAT-010's network-free drain boundary, this layer performs no network I/O;
137	origin-sync and push are out-of-band per FEAT-023.
138	
139	## On-Disk Layout
140	
141	```text
142	.ddx/
143	└── runs/
144	    └── <run-id>/
145	        ├── record.json
146	        ├── prompt.md
147	        ├── result.json
148	        ├── session.jsonl
149	        ├── stdout.log
150	        ├── stderr.log
151	        └── evidence/
152	            ├── manifest.json
153	            └── checks.json
154	```
155	
156	Writers create records in a temporary directory and atomically rename into
157	`.ddx/runs/<run-id>/`. A partially written directory is never published as a
158	complete run. Attachment paths in `record.json` are relative to the run
159	directory.
160	
161	## Legacy Migration
162	
163	Two existing layouts are read during the migration window:
164	
165	- `.ddx/exec-runs/` or `.ddx/exec-runs.d/` records become virtual layer-1
166	  records.
167	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundles become virtual layer-2 records.
168	
169	Migration rules:
170	
171	1. New writes always target `.ddx/runs/`.
172	2. Readers expose legacy records through the same `ddx runs` query model.
173	3. A one-shot rewrite can normalize legacy records into `.ddx/runs/`.
174	4. After the documented cutoff, fallback readers are removed.
175	
176	The migration must preserve git-tracked execute-bead evidence. It must not
177	rewrite historical bead attempt commits or `closing_commit_sha` pointers.
178	
179	## Stop Conditions
180	
181	`ddx work` evaluates stop conditions only between `ddx try` attempts:
182	
183	| Disposition | Trigger |
184	| --- | --- |
185	| `drained` | no ready beads remain |
186	| `blocked` | remaining ready beads are terminal for the current policy |
187	| `deferred` | wall-clock or attempt-count budget exhausted |
188	| `no_progress` | configured consecutive attempts produced no commit and no merged side effect |
189	| `signal` | SIGINT/SIGTERM received; first signal cancels in-flight work cooperatively and stops new claims |
190	| `resource_exhausted` | execution roots are not safely writable or lack required bytes/inodes after cleanup |
191	
192	The layer-3 evaluation log records the candidate bead, previous outcome,
193	retry eligibility, requested power bounds, passthrough envelope presence, and
194	the condition that fired. The log may inspect DDx-owned attempt outcomes and
195	agent typed status; it must not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
196	
197	Signal handling is process-root behavior, not a per-command special case.
198	The root command installs a SIGINT/SIGTERM-backed context. On the first signal,
199	DDx writes `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully` to stderr/stdout visible
200	to the operator, cancels the root context, and lets `ddx work` / `ddx try`
201	finish the TD-031 interruption path: preserve available evidence, remove
202	runtime liveness state, release any non-terminal bead claim, and exit with
203	layer-3 disposition `signal`. A second signal may hard-abort rather than wait
204	for cleanup.
205	
206	## Cleanup Manager
207	
208	The run substrate includes one DDx-owned cleanup manager used by `ddx try`,
209	`ddx work`, server-managed workers, and an explicit operator cleanup command.
210	The manager is scoped to DDx-created execution resources only; it never scans or
211	removes arbitrary temp directories.
212	
213	### Roots and ownership
214	
215	The manager knows the configured temporary worktree root, durable run/evidence
216	roots, worker liveness root, and project git repository. Every created
217	temporary execution directory records enough ownership to answer:
218	
219	- which project created it
220	- which attempt or worker owns it
221	- whether the attempt reached published evidence
222	- whether it was intentionally preserved
223	- when its liveness signal was last refreshed
224	
225	During the legacy migration window, the manager handles both
226	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` and `.ddx/runs/<run-id>` evidence, but it treats
227	complete evidence bundles as durable data rather than scratch.
228	DDx-owned cleanup scope also includes DDx-created test and e2e scratch roots,
229	generated test binaries, and run-state or liveness files. Recognized DDx-owned
230	scratch prefixes are: `ddx-exec-wt`, `ddx-claim-heartbeats`,
231	`ddx-metric-keepalive`, `ddx-test-`, and `ddx-e2e-`. Any directory containing
232	a `cleanup.json` metadata file with matching project ownership is DDx-owned
233	regardless of its name. A path is eligible for deletion when: (a) it has
234	`cleanup.json` metadata and the liveness is expired or the owning attempt is
235	terminal; or (b) it matches a recognized DDx prefix without metadata, the
236	directory mtime is at least **6 hours** old, and no live PID or active session
237	is present. The manager preserves published evidence and registered active
238	worktrees.
239	
240	### Entry points
241	
242	Inline cleanup runs inside `ddx try` before and after worktree lifecycle
243	operations. It removes partial directories from failed setup and removes the
244	isolated worktree after merge, preserve, no-changes, no-evidence, failed
245	publish, or cooperative interruption finalization.
246	
247	Loop cleanup runs inside `ddx work` at startup, between attempts after
248	setup/finalization failure, periodically while polling, and during graceful
249	shutdown. It may clean stale resources from prior attempts before the next
250	claim.
251	
252	Background cleanup runs occasionally while long-lived DDx processes are alive.
253	It uses jitter plus a project cleanup lock so concurrent workers do not all
254	prune at once. It must tolerate another process winning the race to remove a
255	resource.
256	
257	An explicit operator command, such as `ddx cleanup` or `ddx doctor cleanup`,
258	runs the same manager and reports what it removed without requiring a queue
259	drain.
260	
261	### Conservative deletion rules
262	
263	The manager may delete:
264	
265	- unregistered directories under DDx temp worktree roots whose names and
266	  metadata identify DDx ownership and whose liveness is absent or stale
267	- registered git worktrees under DDx temp roots whose owning attempt is
268	  terminal or whose liveness marker is stale
269	- DDx-created test and e2e scratch roots that satisfy the same ownership,
270	  age, and liveness rules
271	- generated test binaries and run-state or liveness files that are DDx-owned
272	- stale heartbeat/liveness files for dead PIDs or expired sessions
273	- partial setup directories that never reached atomic evidence publication
274	- old non-preserved scratch data past configured retention
275	
276	The manager must not delete:
277	
278	- preserved attempt worktrees
279	- local evidence refs under `refs/ddx/iterations/...`
280	- complete `.ddx/runs/<id>` or `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` evidence
281	- active worktrees with a live PID/session heartbeat
282	- paths outside configured DDx roots
283	- paths that only loosely resemble DDx names without matching ownership
284	  metadata or registered worktree evidence
285	
286	### Resource preflight and loop-fatal handling
287	
288	Before `ddx try` claims a bead or creates a worktree, it checks the configured
289	temp worktree root and durable evidence root for writability, free bytes, and
290	free inodes when inode data is available. If preflight fails, `ddx try` runs one
291	immediate cleanup pass and repeats the check. If the check still fails, the
292	attempt returns `resource_exhausted` without claiming the bead.
293	
294	`ddx work` and server-managed workers perform the same cleanup pass before the
295	first claim and before any later claim when any checked temp or evidence root
296	falls below the soft cleanup trigger of **512 MiB free bytes** or **8192 free
297	inodes**. If the temp roots remain below the hard stop floor of **64 MiB free
298	bytes** and **1024 free inodes** after that cleanup, the loop records host
299	exhaustion and stops claiming new beads.
300	
301	If resource exhaustion occurs after a claim or during worktree setup,
302	`ddx try` records whatever partial evidence is available, removes any partial
303	unregistered directory it created, releases the claim if the bead did not close,
304	and returns `resource_exhausted`.
305	
306	`ddx work` treats `resource_exhausted` as host infrastructure failure. It
307	records a layer-3 stop-condition entry, prints an operator-visible message such
308	as `resource exhausted after cleanup; stopping work loop`, and stops claiming
309	new beads. It must not continue scanning the ready queue in the same process.
310	
311	Cleanup output is structured. Routine passes are trace/debug or worker events.
312	Passes that reclaim meaningful bytes or inodes emit an operator-visible summary
313	including counts. Failures include path, class, and whether the failure blocked
314	progress.
315	
316	Expected implementation tests include `TestExecutionCleanup_RemovesStaleDDXScratchDirs`,
317	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_RunsCleanupBelowSoftFloor`, and
318	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_StopsBelowHardFloorAfterCleanup`.
319	
320	## Drill-Down Query Model
321	
322	The read API is centered on `ddx runs`:
323	
324	- `ddx runs list --layer 1|2|3 --bead <id> --artifact <id>`
325	- `ddx runs show <run-id>`
326	- `ddx runs children <run-id>`
327	- `ddx runs parents <run-id>`
328	- `ddx runs log <run-id>`
329	- `ddx runs result <run-id>`
330	
331	Server and MCP surfaces mirror those reads. A layer-3 detail page drills down
332	to child layer-2 attempts; each layer-2 attempt drills down to child layer-1
333	invocations. The graph is navigated by `parent_run_id` and child id arrays, not
334	by path guessing.
335	
336	## Implementation Notes
337	
338	- Reuse the existing execution attachment discipline where it still fits, but
339	  do not keep `.ddx/exec-runs` and `.ddx/executions` as peer authoritative
340	  writers.
341	- Keep passthrough constraints in one request/record subobject so harness,
342	  provider, and model do not spread through queue, retry, or scheduling code.
343	- Treat artifact regeneration as metadata on layer 1 or layer 2, depending on
344	  whether the generator returns content directly or edits the repo in a
345	  worktree.
346	- Add tests at the storage boundary for atomic publish, legacy read
347	  compatibility, parent/child traversal, and no-progress stop-condition
348	  recording.
349

5	    - helix.prd
